data.title: REG - Monks Inv.Trust - Total Voting Rights
data.description:
RNS Number : 6281T Monks Investment Trust PLC 01 August 2025  

The Monks Investment Trust PLC (MNKS)

Legal Entity Identifier: 213800MRI1JTUKG5AF64

Voting Rights and Capital

In conformity with Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rule 5.6.1.R we would like to notify the market of the following:

The Monks Investment Trust PLC capital consists of 253,171,460 ordinary shares. The Monks Investment Trust PLC holds 70,344,794 ordinary shares in Treasury. Therefore, the total number of voting rights in The Monks Investment Trust PLC is 182,826,666, as at 31 July 2025.

The above figure (182,826,666) may be used by shareholders as the denominator for the calculations by which they will determine if they are required to notify their interest in, or a change to their interest in, The Monks Investment Trust PLC under the FCA's Disclosure Guidance and Transparency Rules.

Baillie Gifford & Co Limited

Company Secretaries

1 August 2025

Regulated Information Classification: Total number of voting rights and capital

This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. data.title: India's PNB Housing Finance on track for steepest drop in 7 years as CEO resigns
data.description:
Shares of India's PNB Housing Finance were on course for their worst single-session fall in nearly seven years on Friday, as its CEO's surprise resignation sparked concerns over the growth of its profitable affordable housing segment.

The home financier's shares fell more than 17% to a four-month low of 813 rupees in Mumbai and were on track for their sharpest fall since October 19, 2018. India's benchmark Nifty 50 index fell 0.6% in the wake of steep U.S. tariffs.

Girish Kousgi, who was named PNB Housing's MD and CEO in October 2022, will step down from his post on October 28 this year, the lender said after market hours on Thursday, adding that the search for a successor has kicked off.

The lender's strategic shift towards affordable and emerging housing had led to analysts raising their ratings on its shares as competition in the segment is relatively benign, according to Pranav Gundlapalle, senior research analyst at Bernstein.

"The CEO - who came from an affordable housing background at Can Fin Homes - was widely seen as the architect of this transformation. His resignation now casts doubt on the continuity of this strategy," Gundlapalle said.

If the affordable housing focus were to be unwound, PNB Housing risks becoming like other housing finance companies, which face significant challenges in competing with banks, according to the analyst.

SBI Securities said the CEO's resignation is a negative for the stock in the short term.

PNB Housing Finance's assets in the affordable housing segment more than doubled year-on-year to 57.44 billion rupees ($656.86 million) for the quarter ended June 30.

On Thursday, the housing finance company said that its "strategic priorities, business focus, and growth trajectory remain firmly intact, based on the strong foundation that Kousgi has helped build".

Nearly 30 million shares changed hands as of 2:47 p.m. IST, 27 times the 30-day average.

($1 = 87.4460 Indian rupees)

data.title: FTSE Straits Times Index Closes 0.5% Lower at 4153.83
Eurozone Bond Yields Remain at Higher Levels After Inflation Data — Market Talk
data.description: Eurozone government bonds yields remain higher on the day after flash estimate July eurozone inflation data came in slightly above expectations. Headline annual inflation was 2.0%, above analysts' forecast of 1.9% in The Wall Street Journal's poll. Annual core inflation matched expectations of 2.3%. "Overall inflation is likely to remain above the European Central Bank's expectations in the coming quarters, making further interest-rate cuts by the central bank unlikely," says Commerzbank's senior economist Vincent Stamer in a note. The 10-year Bund yield is up 2 basis points at 2.711% after the data, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: China's Gaming Companies Could Further Diversify Top-Up Channels — Market Talk
data.description: China's gaming companies could further diversify account top-up channels to cut costs, HSBC analysts say in a research note. Chinese game makers have ramped up efforts to get users to top up their wallets to spend on mobile games through platforms such as WeChat and company websites rather than iOS and Android app stores to cut costs. Android app stores typically charge channel costs of up to 50% of grossing, while iOS app stores charge 30%. HSBC estimates payments from alternative channels have accounted for around 40%-50% of total mobile game grossing, and that this trend could continue. Meanwhile, they reckon shooting games could have more room to grow in China, with Tencent, which is a leading player in the genre, set to benefit. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Italy's Safilo jumps after Q2 earnings beat
data.description:
** Shares in Safilo jump around 9% after the Italian eyewear group reported a beat on its second-quarter earnings

** Equita notes that adjusted EBITDA rose 9% to 27.9 million euros, beating expectations for flat performance, on stable costs and improved channel/product mix

** The broker reaffirms its "buy" rating on the stock and lifts its PT by 7% to 1.60 euros, citing strong Q2 profitability and resilient sales

** Equita adds that Safilo does not see the need for further price increases to manage tariffs impact, with price hikes so far also causing no negative reaction

** Still, it notes "the impacts of an inflationary scenario on consumption will have to be monitored"

($1 = 0.8764 euros)

data.title: Eurozone Inflation Holds Steady, Reinforcing ECB Rate Hold Views
data.description:
By Ed Frankl

The eurozone's annual rate of inflation was steady at the European Central Bank's target in July, a surprise likely to underpin expectations that policymakers will leave their key interest unchanged next month.

Consumer prices were 2.0% higher than the same month of last year, matching the inflation rate of June, European Union data agency Eurostat said Friday. That was a little stronger than consensus expectations from economists polled by The Wall Street Journal for inflation to cool to 1.9%.

The steady price growth in July comes after economic growth in the 20-nation eurozone economy has held better than expected. Economic activity increased in the second quarter, counter to expectations that it would contract slightly.

U.S. import duties had been expected to significantly dampen Europe's economic activity in recent months. In the first quarter of the year, the economy was lifted as U.S. firms built stocks of imported goods ahead of President Trump's tariff announcements in early April.

That second-quarter resilience prompted Barclays economists to join others in saying they no longer expect an ECB interest-rate cut in September, according to a note to clients on Thursday.

The Frankfurt-based central bank kept its key rate on hold at 2% last week after eight interest-rate cuts since June 2024, the reversal of its cycle of rate hikes to combat spiralling inflation after the pandemic and Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

Eurozone inflation has been at or below the ECB's target now since May, with bank staff forecasts anticipating it will ease to 1.6% on average in 2026 but return to target in 2027.

Its President Christine Lagarde said at last week's monetary-policy meeting that the bank was in "a good place" regarding inflation, and said they would not respond to "some minor deviation" from the target such as the expected 2026 undershoot.

Economists believe U.S. tariffs will dampen inflation in the eurozone by demand for goods made in Europe, while also prompting Chinese businesses to cut their prices as they search for alternatives to a U.S. market where they face sharply higher tariffs.

The appreciation of the euro could also lead to lower inflation, given they make imports into the eurozone cheaper, a prospect that prompted France's central bank governor Francois Villeroy de Galhau to warn that the ECB must be open to lowering rates.

While the trade agreement announced on Sunday between the U.S. and EU raises tariff barriers well beyond what they were at the start of the year, they also lessen market uncertainty, likely helping allow businesses to plan and invest and maintaining some resilience in activity.

Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com
